revision.text:
{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese god of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
He is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==See Also== * {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}
==Notes== {{notelist}}
==References== {{reflist|draft}}
==Bibliography== {{Commons Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category}} {{Refbegin}} *{{cite book|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|title=A Translation of the "Ko-ji-ki," or a record of ancient matters|orig-year=1882|year=1919|url=http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm|series=Transactions of the Asiatic Society of Japan|volume=X}} **{{cite book|last=武田|first=祐吉 (Yūkichi Takeda)|editor=中村啓信|title=新訂古事記|publisher=講談社|orig-year=1977|year=1996|isbn=4-04-400101-4|pages=60, 62, 77, 78, 95}} *{{cite book|last=Aston|first=William George|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|volume=1|place=London|publisher=Japan Society of London|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|author-link=William George Aston}}, English translation **{{cite book|last=宇治谷|first=孟 (Tsutomu Ujitani)|title=日本書紀|volume=上|publisher=講談社|year=1988|isbn=9780802150585}} {{Refend}}

The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese god of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
He is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese god of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
He is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref>
==See Also== * {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}

He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> ==Overview==
{{Infobox deity | type = shinto | name = Amano Hachio-no-Kami | image = | caption = | god_of = Weaving, Textiles | other_names = Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto, Toyohachi-no-Mikoto, Amanoha-no-Mikoto, Amanohaduchi-no-Mikoto, Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto | affiliation = [[Amatsukami]] | parents = | siblings = | consort = | children = | region = Japan | texts = [[Kogo Shūi]], [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Shinsen Shōjiroku]] | shrines = [[Shizuhiko Shrine]], [[Shizuhiko Shrine]], [[Katsuragi Shizuhiko Shrine]] }}
'''Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' ({{nihongo|天羽槌雄神|Amano Hachio-no-Kami}}), also known as **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto** (建葉槌命) or **Shidori-no-Kami** (倭文神), is a deity in **Japanese mythology**, worshiped as the **god of weaving** and textiles.
Amano Hachio-no-Kami is mentioned in **Shinto texts**, including the **Kogo Shūi** (古語拾遺) and the **Nihon Shoki** (日本書紀), and is the **ancestral deity** of the **Shidori (倭文) clan**, an ancient lineage associated with **textile production** in Japan.
## **Names and Titles** Amano Hachio-no-Kami is known by several different names across historical records: - **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto** (建葉槌命) – as recorded in the *Nihon Shoki* - **Toyohachi-no-Mikoto** (止与波知命) – from the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* - **Amanoha-no-Mikoto** (天破命) – from genealogies of the Inbe clan - **Amanohaduchi-no-Mikoto** (天羽雷雄命) – from the *Awa-no-Kuni Inbe Keizu* (*Genealogy of the Inbe Clan of Awa*) - **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto** (武羽槌命) – found in some later texts
The deity is primarily worshiped as the **god of weaving and textiles**, but also plays a role in **Japanese mythology and celestial conflicts**.
## **Mythological Role** ### **Weaving for the Sun Goddess** According to legend, **Amano Hachio-no-Kami** was responsible for weaving the **sacred cloth (Shidori-ori, 倭文織)** used in the rituals of **Amaterasu**, the sun goddess. This fabric was significant in the **ritual to lure Amaterasu out of the heavenly rock cave** (*Amano-Iwato*, 天の岩戸), a pivotal moment in Shinto mythology.
### **Defeat of the Evil Star Deity** In another myth recorded in the *Nihon Shoki*, **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto** played a role in defeating the **evil star deity, Kagaseo (香香背男)**, also known as **Amatsu-Mika-Boshi (天津甕星)**. This battle took place before the **descent of Ninigi-no-Mikoto** (天孫降臨), the ancestor of the **imperial family of Japan**.
The reasons for a **weaving deity fighting a star deity** are unclear, but scholars propose the following interpretations: 1. **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto as a Warrior Deity** - The **"Take" (武)** in the name suggests a **martial** aspect, implying that this deity may have been worshiped as a **guardian god** or warrior deity. 2. **Symbolic Weaving of the Stars** - Another theory posits that **Amano Hachio-no-Kami used weaving magic to trap the star deity Kagaseo**, effectively "binding" him within fabric, symbolizing control over celestial chaos. 3. **Defection and Infiltration Theory** - Some interpretations suggest that **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto was originally aligned with Kagaseo but was persuaded to defect**, causing the downfall of the star god from within.
## **Shrines Dedicated to Amano Hachio-no-Kami** Amano Hachio-no-Kami is enshrined at several Shinto shrines, particularly those related to **weaving and textile production**: - **Shizuhiko Shrine (静神社)** – a major shrine dedicated to the deity. - **Shizuhiko Shrine (倭文神社)** – located in **Tottori Prefecture**. - **Katsuragi Shizuhiko Shrine (葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社)** – in **Nara Prefecture**, historically associated with the Shidori clan.
## **Worship and Influence** - Amano Hachio-no-Kami is revered as the **patron deity of the textile industry**, and shrines dedicated to the deity were closely associated with **weaving guilds and artisans** throughout Japan. - The deity also has connections to **Shinto priests and ritual practices**, particularly those involving **sacred cloth** (*Shidori-ori*), which was considered essential in court rituals and imperial ceremonies.
## **Genealogy and Ancestry** The deity is often regarded as the **divine ancestor of the Shidori (倭文) clan**, a powerful group historically involved in weaving and textile production. Other **related clans** include: - **Nagahata-be (長幡部)** - **Tanabe Sukune (田辺宿禰)** - **Takehara Clan (竹原氏)** - **Mino Clan (美努連)** - **Emishi Clan (神宮部造)** - **Inukai Clan (県犬養氏)**
## **Cultural Influence** ### **Amano Hachio-no-Kami in Popular Culture** The deity is referenced in **modern media and literature**: - **"Your Name" (君の名は, 2016 Anime Film)** - The fictional **Miyamizu Shrine** in the movie *Your Name* is said to enshrine **Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto**, connecting the deity to **Shinto astronomy and fate**.

== '''Worship and Influence''' - Amano Hachio-no-Kami is revered as the '''patron deity of the textile industry''', and shrines dedicated to the deity were closely associated with '''weaving guilds and artisans''' throughout Japan. - The deity also has connections to '''Shinto priests and ritual practices''', particularly those involving '''sacred cloth''' (*Shidori-ori*), which was considered essential in court rituals and imperial ceremonies.
== '''Genealogy and Ancestry''' The deity is often regarded as the '''divine ancestor of the Shidori (倭文) clan''', a powerful group historically involved in weaving and textile production. Other '''related clans''' include: - '''Nagahata-be (長幡部)''' - '''Tanabe Sukune (田辺宿禰)''' - '''Takehara Clan (竹原氏)''' - '''Mino Clan (美努連)''' - '''Emishi Clan (神宮部造)''' - '''Inukai Clan (県犬養氏)'''
== '''Cultural Influence''' === '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami in Popular Culture''' The deity is referenced in '''modern media and literature''': - '''"Your Name" (君の名は, 2016 Anime Film)''' - The fictional '''Miyamizu Shrine''' in the movie *Your Name* is said to enshrine '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''', connecting the deity to '''Shinto astronomy and fate'''.

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese god of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
He is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> ==Overview==
{{Infobox deity | type = shinto | name = Amano Hachio-no-Kami | image = | caption = | god_of = Weaving, Textiles | other_names = Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto, Toyohachi-no-Mikoto, Amanoha-no-Mikoto, Amanohaduchi-no-Mikoto, Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto | affiliation = [[Amatsukami]] | parents = | siblings = | consort = | children = | region = Japan | texts = [[Kogo Shūi]], [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Shinsen Shōjiroku]] | shrines = [[Shizuhiko Shrine]], [[Shizuhiko Shrine]], [[Katsuragi Shizuhiko Shrine]] }}
'''Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' ({{nihongo|天羽槌雄神|Amano Hachio-no-Kami}}), also known as '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' (建葉槌命) or '''Shidori-no-Kami''' (倭文神), is a deity in '''Japanese mythology''', worshiped as the '''god of weaving''' and textiles.
Amano Hachio-no-Kami is mentioned in '''Shinto texts''', including the '''Kogo Shūi''' (古語拾遺) and the '''Nihon Shoki''' (日本書紀), and is the '''ancestral deity''' of the '''Shidori (倭文) clan''', an ancient lineage associated with '''textile production''' in Japan.
== '''Names and Titles''' Amano Hachio-no-Kami is known by several different names across historical records: - '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' (建葉槌命) – as recorded in the *Nihon Shoki* - '''Toyohachi-no-Mikoto''' (止与波知命) – from the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* - '''Amanoha-no-Mikoto''' (天破命) – from genealogies of the Inbe clan - '''Amanohaduchi-no-Mikoto''' (天羽雷雄命) – from the *Awa-no-Kuni Inbe Keizu* (*Genealogy of the Inbe Clan of Awa*) - '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' (武羽槌命) – found in some later texts
The deity is primarily worshiped as the '''god of weaving and textiles''', but also plays a role in '''Japanese mythology and celestial conflicts'''.
== '''Mythological Role''' === '''Weaving for the Sun Goddess''' According to legend, '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' was responsible for weaving the '''sacred cloth (Shidori-ori, 倭文織)''' used in the rituals of '''Amaterasu''', the sun goddess. This fabric was significant in the '''ritual to lure Amaterasu out of the heavenly rock cave''' (*Amano-Iwato*, 天の岩戸), a pivotal moment in Shinto mythology.
=== '''Defeat of the Evil Star Deity''' In another myth recorded in the *Nihon Shoki*, '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' played a role in defeating the '''evil star deity, Kagaseo (香香背男)''', also known as '''Amatsu-Mika-Boshi (天津甕星)'''. This battle took place before the '''descent of Ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天孫降臨), the ancestor of the '''imperial family of Japan'''.
The reasons for a '''weaving deity fighting a star deity''' are unclear, but scholars propose the following interpretations: 1. '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto as a Warrior Deity''' - The '''"Take" (武)''' in the name suggests a '''martial''' aspect, implying that this deity may have been worshiped as a '''guardian god''' or warrior deity. 2. '''Symbolic Weaving of the Stars''' - Another theory posits that '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami used weaving magic to trap the star deity Kagaseo''', effectively "binding" him within fabric, symbolizing control over celestial chaos. 3. '''Defection and Infiltration Theory''' - Some interpretations suggest that '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto was originally aligned with Kagaseo but was persuaded to defect''', causing the downfall of the star god from within.
== '''Shrines Dedicated to Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' Amano Hachio-no-Kami is enshrined at several Shinto shrines, particularly those related to '''weaving and textile production''': - '''Shizuhiko Shrine (静神社)''' – a major shrine dedicated to the deity. - '''Shizuhiko Shrine (倭文神社)''' – located in '''Tottori Prefecture'''. - '''Katsuragi Shizuhiko Shrine (葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社)''' – in '''Nara Prefecture''', historically associated with the Shidori clan.
== '''Worship and Influence''' - Amano Hachio-no-Kami is revered as the '''patron deity of the textile industry''', and shrines dedicated to the deity were closely associated with '''weaving guilds and artisans''' throughout Japan. - The deity also has connections to '''Shinto priests and ritual practices''', particularly those involving '''sacred cloth''' (*Shidori-ori*), which was considered essential in court rituals and imperial ceremonies.
== '''Genealogy and Ancestry''' The deity is often regarded as the '''divine ancestor of the Shidori (倭文) clan''', a powerful group historically involved in weaving and textile production. Other '''related clans''' include: - '''Nagahata-be (長幡部)''' - '''Tanabe Sukune (田辺宿禰)''' - '''Takehara Clan (竹原氏)''' - '''Mino Clan (美努連)''' - '''Emishi Clan (神宮部造)''' - '''Inukai Clan (県犬養氏)'''
== '''Cultural Influence''' === '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami in Popular Culture''' The deity is referenced in '''modern media and literature''': - '''"Your Name" (君の名は, 2016 Anime Film)''' - The fictional '''Miyamizu Shrine''' in the movie *Your Name* is said to enshrine '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''', connecting the deity to '''Shinto astronomy and fate'''.

{{Infobox deity | type = shinto | name = Amano Hachio-no-Kami | image = | caption = | god_of = Weaving, Textiles | other_names = Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto, Toyohachi-no-Mikoto, Amanoha-no-Mikoto, Amanohaduchi-no-Mikoto, Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto | affiliation = [[Amatsukami]] | parents = | siblings = | consort = | children = | region = Japan | texts = [[Kogo Shūi]], [[Nihon Shoki]], [[Shinsen Shōjiroku]] | shrines = [[Shizuhiko Shrine]], [[Shizuhiko Shrine]], [[Katsuragi Shizuhiko Shrine]] }}
'''Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' ({{nihongo|天羽槌雄神|Amano Hachio-no-Kami}}), also known as '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' (建葉槌命) or '''Shidori-no-Kami''' (倭文神), is a deity in '''Japanese mythology''', worshiped as the '''god of weaving''' and textiles.
Amano Hachio-no-Kami is mentioned in '''Shinto texts''', including the '''Kogo Shūi''' (古語拾遺) and the '''Nihon Shoki''' (日本書紀), and is the '''ancestral deity''' of the '''Shidori (倭文) clan''', an ancient lineage associated with '''textile production''' in Japan.
== '''Names and Titles''' == Amano Hachio-no-Kami is known by several different names across historical records: - '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' (建葉槌命) – as recorded in the *Nihon Shoki* - '''Toyohachi-no-Mikoto''' (止与波知命) – from the *Shinsen Shōjiroku* - '''Amanoha-no-Mikoto''' (天破命) – from genealogies of the Inbe clan - '''Amanohaduchi-no-Mikoto''' (天羽雷雄命) – from the *Awa-no-Kuni Inbe Keizu* (*Genealogy of the Inbe Clan of Awa*) - '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' (武羽槌命) – found in some later texts
The deity is primarily worshiped as the '''god of weaving and textiles''', but also plays a role in '''Japanese mythology and celestial conflicts'''.
== '''Mythological Role''' ==
=== '''Weaving for the Sun Goddess''' === According to legend, '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' was responsible for weaving the '''sacred cloth (Shidori-ori, 倭文織)''' used in the rituals of '''Amaterasu''', the sun goddess. This fabric was significant in the '''ritual to lure Amaterasu out of the heavenly rock cave''' (*Amano-Iwato*, 天の岩戸), a pivotal moment in Shinto mythology.
=== '''Defeat of the Evil Star Deity''' === In another myth recorded in the *Nihon Shoki*, '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''' played a role in defeating the '''evil star deity, Kagaseo (香香背男)''', also known as '''Amatsu-Mika-Boshi (天津甕星)'''. This battle took place before the '''descent of Ninigi-no-Mikoto''' (天孫降臨), the ancestor of the '''imperial family of Japan'''.
The reasons for a '''weaving deity fighting a star deity''' are unclear, but scholars propose the following interpretations: 1. '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto as a Warrior Deity''' - The '''"Take" (武)''' in the name suggests a '''martial''' aspect, implying that this deity may have been worshiped as a '''guardian god''' or warrior deity. 2. '''Symbolic Weaving of the Stars''' - Another theory posits that '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami used weaving magic to trap the star deity Kagaseo''', effectively "binding" him within fabric, symbolizing control over celestial chaos. 3. '''Defection and Infiltration Theory''' - Some interpretations suggest that '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto was originally aligned with Kagaseo but was persuaded to defect''', causing the downfall of the star god from within.
== '''Shrines Dedicated to Amano Hachio-no-Kami''' == Amano Hachio-no-Kami is enshrined at several Shinto shrines, particularly those related to '''weaving and textile production''': - '''Shizuhiko Shrine (静神社)''' – a major shrine dedicated to the deity. - '''Shizuhiko Shrine (倭文神社)''' – located in '''Tottori Prefecture'''. - '''Katsuragi Shizuhiko Shrine (葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社)''' – in '''Nara Prefecture''', historically associated with the Shidori clan.
== '''Worship and Influence''' == - Amano Hachio-no-Kami is revered as the '''patron deity of the textile industry''', and shrines dedicated to the deity were closely associated with '''weaving guilds and artisans''' throughout Japan. - The deity also has connections to '''Shinto priests and ritual practices''', particularly those involving '''sacred cloth''' (*Shidori-ori*), which was considered essential in court rituals and imperial ceremonies.
== '''Genealogy and Ancestry''' == The deity is often regarded as the '''divine ancestor of the Shidori (倭文) clan''', a powerful group historically involved in weaving and textile production. Other '''related clans''' include: - '''Nagahata-be (長幡部)''' - '''Tanabe Sukune (田辺宿禰)''' - '''Takehara Clan (竹原氏)''' - '''Mino Clan (美努連)''' - '''Emishi Clan (神宮部造)''' - '''Inukai Clan (県犬養氏)'''
== '''Cultural Influence''' ==
=== '''Amano Hachio-no-Kami in Popular Culture''' The deity is referenced in '''modern media and literature''': - '''"Your Name" (君の名は, 2016 Anime Film)''' === - The fictional '''Miyamizu Shrine''' in the movie *Your Name* is said to enshrine '''Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto''', connecting the deity to '''Shinto astronomy and fate'''.

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shizuri Shrines|ja|倭文神社}} {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese god of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
He is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> ==Overview==

He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Overview ==
Ameno Hazuchio no Kami is mentioned in the ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'' as ''Ameno Hazuchio no Kami''. In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', she is referred to as {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}} and {{ill|Shidori deity|ja|倭文神}}. In the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'', she is called {{ill|Toyohachi-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波知命}} in the Yamashiro Province section and {{ill|Amenoha-no-Mikoto|ja|天破命}} in another entry. The ''Anwa Province Imbe Family Genealogy'' (安房国忌部家系), which includes the "Ibe no Sukune Honkeicho" (齋部宿祢本系帳), records her name as {{ill|Ameno Haikazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|天羽雷雄命}} and also lists {{ill|Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|武羽槌命}} and {{ill|Toyohazuji-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波豆知命}} as alternative names.<ref>{{cite book|和書 |title=安房国忌部家系|chapter=齋部宿祢本系帳|section=天羽雷雄命|doi=10.11501/2538213|url={{NDLDC|2538213/55}} }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
She is said to have woven sacred cloth (''aya'') to lure {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} out of the {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天の岩戸}}. The cloth she wove is called {{ill|Shidori cloth|ja|倭文布}} or simply ''Shidori'' (倭文), a term referring to a type of woven fabric. Other weaving deities include {{ill|Takuhatachichihime-no-Mikoto|ja|栲幡千々姫命}} and {{ill|Amanotatakahime-no-Mikoto|ja|天棚機姫神}}, but Ameno Hazuchio no Kami is revered as the **supreme ancestor of weaving**. She is also the **ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}**.
She is worshipped as the **goddess of weaving** in **{{ill|Shidori Shrines|ja|倭文神社}}**, **{{ill|Shizuka Shrine|ja|静神社}}**, **Hattori Shrines**, and others throughout Japan.
== Genealogy ==
In ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'', she is called the **far ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}**. In ''{{ill|Sendai Kujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', she is described as the **distant ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori-no-Mikoto|ja|倭文造}}**.
== Legends and Deeds ==
Ameno Hazuchio no Kami appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' under the name {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}}. She is said to have **subjugated the star deity** **{{ill|Amanomikaboshi|ja|天津甕星}} (also known as Kagaseo no Kami)**, whom **{{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami|ja|武甕槌命}}** and **{{ill|Futsunushi-no-Kami|ja|経津主神}}** were unable to pacify.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=植松安|date=1920-06|title=仮名日本書紀|volume=上巻|pages=87–88|publisher=大同館書店|url={{NDLDC|961006/117}} |doi=10.11501/961006 }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> == See Also ==
* {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * ''{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は。}}'' (2016 anime film), where **{{ill|Miyamizu Shrine|ja|宮水神社}}** is depicted as enshrining **{{ill|Shidori deity Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|倭文神 建葉槌命}}**. * {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} * [[List of Japanese deities]] * [[:en:Textile_production_in_Japan|Textile production in Japan]] * [[:en:Shinto_cosmology|Shinto cosmology]] * [[:en:Weaving_in_mythology|Weaving in mythology]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shizuri Shrines|ja|倭文神社}} {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese god of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
He is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
He decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
He is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it he may either have also been a war god based on the "take" in his name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that he wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Overview ==
Takehazuchi no Kami is mentioned in the ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'' as ''Takehazuchi no Kami''. In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', she is referred to as {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}} and {{ill|Shidori deity|ja|倭文神}}. In the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'', she is called {{ill|Toyohachi-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波知命}} in the Yamashiro Province section and {{ill|Amenoha-no-Mikoto|ja|天破命}} in another entry. The ''Anwa Province Imbe Family Genealogy'' (安房国忌部家系), which includes the "Ibe no Sukune Honkeicho" (齋部宿祢本系帳), records her name as {{ill|Ameno Haikazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|天羽雷雄命}} and also lists {{ill|Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|武羽槌命}} and {{ill|Toyohazuji-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波豆知命}} as alternative names.<ref>{{cite book|和書 |title=安房国忌部家系|chapter=齋部宿祢本系帳|section=天羽雷雄命|doi=10.11501/2538213|url={{NDLDC|2538213/55}} }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
She is said to have woven sacred cloth (''aya'') to lure {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} out of the {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天の岩戸}}. The cloth she wove is called {{ill|Shidori cloth|ja|倭文布}} or simply ''Shidori'' (倭文), a term referring to a type of woven fabric. Other weaving deities include {{ill|Takuhatachichihime-no-Mikoto|ja|栲幡千々姫命}} and {{ill|Amanotatakahime-no-Mikoto|ja|天棚機姫神}}, but Takehazuchi no Kami is revered as the **supreme ancestor of weaving**. She is also the **ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}**.
She is worshipped as the **goddess of weaving** in **{{ill|Shidori Shrines|ja|倭文神社}}**, **{{ill|Shizuka Shrine|ja|静神社}}**, **Hattori Shrines**, and others throughout Japan.
== Genealogy ==
In ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'', she is called the **far ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}**. In ''{{ill|Sendai Kujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', she is described as the **distant ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori-no-Mikoto|ja|倭文造}}**.
== Legends and Deeds ==
Takehazuchi no Kami appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' under the name {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}}. She is said to have **subjugated the star deity** **{{ill|Amanomikaboshi|ja|天津甕星}} (also known as Kagaseo no Kami)**, whom **{{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami|ja|武甕槌命}}** and **{{ill|Futsunushi-no-Kami|ja|経津主神}}** were unable to pacify.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=植松安|date=1920-06|title=仮名日本書紀|volume=上巻|pages=87–88|publisher=大同館書店|url={{NDLDC|961006/117}} |doi=10.11501/961006 }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
==Descendant clans==
{{ill|Nagahata Clan|ja|長幡部連}}, {{ill|Shidori Clan|ja|倭文氏}}, {{ill|Tanabe Clan|ja|田辺宿禰}}, {{ill|Takehara Clan|ja|竹原氏}}, {{ill|Okura Clan|ja|巨椋連}}, {{ill|Ōkura Okihajime Clan|ja|大椋置始連}}, {{ill|Imaki Clan|ja|今木連}}, {{ill|Yuge Clan|ja|弓削氏}}, {{ill|Tame Clan|ja|多米連}}, {{ill|Minu Clan|ja|美努連}} ({{ill|Minokuni Rulers|ja|三野県主}}), {{ill|Tottoribe Clan|ja|鳥取部連}}, {{ill|Jingu Clan|ja|神宮部造}}, {{ill|Agatanoinukai Clan|ja|県犬養氏}}
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> == See Also ==
* {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * ''{{ill|Your Name|ja|君の名は。}}'' (2016 anime film), where **{{ill|Miyamizu Shrine|ja|宮水神社}}** is depicted as enshrining **{{ill|Shidori deity Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|倭文神 建葉槌命}}**. * {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} * [[List of Japanese deities]] * [[:en:Textile_production_in_Japan|Textile production in Japan]] * [[:en:Shinto_cosmology|Shinto cosmology]] * [[:en:Weaving_in_mythology|Weaving in mythology]]

She is said to have '''subjugated the star deity''' '''{{ill|Amanomikaboshi|ja|天津甕星}} (also known as Kagaseo no Kami)''', whom '''{{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami|ja|武甕槌命}}''' and '''{{ill|Futsunushi-no-Kami|ja|経津主神}}''' were unable to pacify.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=植松安|date=1920-06|title=仮名日本書紀|volume=上巻|pages=87–88|publisher=大同館書店|url={{NDLDC|961006/117}} |doi=10.11501/961006 }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>

== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> == See Also ==

== '''External Links''' == * [https://www.jinja-honcho.or.jp/ Shrine Association of Japan] * [[:ja:天羽槌雄神|Japanese Wikipedia: Amano Hachio-no-Kami]] {{Shizuri Faith}}{{Shinto-stub}} [[fr:Utilisateur:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi]]

{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{Infobox deity |image = Bentenjima on Inasa Beach.jpg|right|thumb|250x250px| caption = Inasa Beach ([[:ja:稲佐の浜|稲佐の浜]] ''Inasa-no-hama'') in {{ill|Izumo, Shimane|Izumo|en}}, {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en}}, where the myth places Ōkuninushi's meeting with the messengers of heaven and his surrender to them.|cult_centre={{ill|Shizuri Shrines|ja|倭文神社}} {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} {{ill|Shizu Shrine|ja|静神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shizumi Shrine }} {{ill|Katsuraki Shitori ni Imasu Ame no Ha Ikazuchi no Mikoto Shrine|ja|葛木倭文座天羽雷命神社}}|type=Shinto|name=Takehazuchi|script_name=天羽槌雄神|other_names=Ama no Hazuchi|father={{ill|Amanohiwashi no Mikoto|ja|天日鷲神|simple|user:immanuelle/Amanohiwashi no Mikoto}}}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} (天羽槌雄神){{efn|also read as Amenohazuchi}} is the Japanese goddess of weaving<ref name="Nihongi6970">{{cite wikisource|author=Aston|date=1896|Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|chapter=Book II|pages=69–70}}</ref> Also known as Shizuri.<ref name="mizue-2">{{Cite web |last=Mizue |first=Mori |title=Shizuri |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9154 |website={{ill|Kokugakuin University|en}} Encyclopedia of Shinto}}</ref>
She is believed to be a fifth generation descendant of {{ill|Takamimusubi|en}} most likely through [[Sukunabikona]] due to the other associations of him.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=天羽槌雄神（あめのはづちのおのかみ） ・建葉槌命（たけはづちのみこと）／日本の神々の話 : 四季歩のつれづれ|url=http://tamtom.blog44.fc2.com/blog-entry-2646.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=tamtom.blog44.fc2.com}}</ref>
She decorated the streamers on the [[Masakaki]] tree used to lure out [[Amaterasu]] from the cave.<ref>{{Cite web|title=神々の庭：天羽槌雄神 (あめのはずちおのかみ)|url=http://shinto.fugarden.com/gosaishin_amenohazutio.html|access-date=2024-05-23|website=shinto.fugarden.com}}</ref>
She is a warrior who vanquished the rebellious star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} during the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}}.<ref name="宇治谷 1988">{{Harvnb|宇治谷|1988}} 『日本書紀』上 p.64, 58</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /><ref name="mizue-2" /> [[ja:天羽槌雄神]][[en:draft:Takehazuchi]]
Appended to the two passages of the subjugation of the earth in the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] and the [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] is the mention of a star deity named {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|en}} who resisted till the end, and whom {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} and {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}} were particularly eager to vanquish. The latter passage states that the being who subdued the star god, referred to as {{Nihongo|''Iwai no nushi''|斎の大人}} is enshrined at Katori, hinting that it might be {{ill|Futsunushi|en|4=fr}}.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=h98EAAAAMAAJ|title=和漢三才図会|author=寺島良安|author2=島田勇雄, 樋口元巳|publisher=平凡社|year=1985|isbn=9784582804478|volume=10|page=71}} subscribes to this view</ref><ref name="Nihongi6970" /> However, the earlier passage says a god named {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft||fr}} was the vanquisher of the star god.<ref name="宇治谷 1988" /><ref>{{Cite web|date=2024-03-20|title=Hoshinokamikakaseo {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240320033035/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9400|access-date=2024-03-20|website=web.archive.org}}</ref>
After Ōkuninushi's assent and withdrawal, the two messengers proceeded to destroy everyone and everything who refused to submit to their authority. They then send the god of weaving, Takehazuchi (建葉槌), to subdue the star god {{ill|Amatsu-Mikaboshi|lt=Kagaseo|en}} (香香背男), the last remaining rebel against Takamagahara (''Nihon Shoki'').<ref name="Nihongi6970" /> After the resistance was gone the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|en}} could begin.<ref name="mizue-2" />
It is believed it She may either have also been a war goddess based on the "take" in her name analogous to {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|4=fr}} or that She wove the stars into fabric, taming them.<ref name=":0" />
The {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en}} identifies him with Amenohatsuchio the ancestor of the Shizuri clan who did weaving.<ref name="mizue-2" />
At {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en|4=fr|5=Shitori-jinja}} {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} and {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}} are worshipped as weaving gods<ref name="mizue-2" />.
== Overview ==
Takehazuchi no Kami is mentioned in the ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'' as ''Takehazuchi no Kami''. In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', she is referred to as {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}} and {{ill|Shidori deity|ja|倭文神}}. In the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'', she is called {{ill|Toyohachi-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波知命}} in the Yamashiro Province section and {{ill|Amenoha-no-Mikoto|ja|天破命}} in another entry. The ''Anwa Province Imbe Family Genealogy'' (安房国忌部家系), which includes the "Ibe no Sukune Honkeicho" (齋部宿祢本系帳), records her name as {{ill|Ameno Haikazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|天羽雷雄命}} and also lists {{ill|Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|武羽槌命}} and {{ill|Toyohazuji-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波豆知命}} as alternative names.<ref>{{cite book|和書 |title=安房国忌部家系|chapter=齋部宿祢本系帳|section=天羽雷雄命|doi=10.11501/2538213|url={{NDLDC|2538213/55}} }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
She is said to have woven sacred cloth (''aya'') to lure {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} out of the {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天の岩戸}}. The cloth she wove is called {{ill|Shidori cloth|ja|倭文布}} or simply ''Shidori'' (倭文), a term referring to a type of woven fabric. Other weaving deities include {{ill|Takuhatachichihime-no-Mikoto|ja|栲幡千々姫命}} and {{ill|Amanotatakahime-no-Mikoto|ja|天棚機姫神}}, but Takehazuchi no Kami is revered as the '''supreme ancestor of weaving'''. She is also the '''ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}'''.
She is worshipped as the '''goddess of weaving''' in '''{{ill|Shidori Shrines|ja|倭文神社}}''', '''{{ill|Shizuka Shrine|ja|静神社}}''', '''Hattori Shrines''', and others throughout Japan.
== Genealogy ==
In ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'', she is called the '''far ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}'''. In ''{{ill|Sendai Kujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', she is described as the '''distant ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori-no-Mikoto|ja|倭文造}}'''.
== Legends and Deeds ==
Takehazuchi no Kami appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' under the name {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}}. She is said to have '''subjugated the star deity''' '''{{ill|Amanomikaboshi|ja|天津甕星}} (also known as Kagaseo no Kami)''', whom '''{{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami|ja|武甕槌命}}''' and '''{{ill|Futsunushi-no-Kami|ja|経津主神}}''' were unable to pacify.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=植松安|date=1920-06|title=仮名日本書紀|volume=上巻|pages=87–88|publisher=大同館書店|url={{NDLDC|961006/117}} |doi=10.11501/961006 }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
==Descendant clans==
{{ill|Nagahata Clan|ja|長幡部連}}, {{ill|Shidori Clan|ja|倭文氏}}, {{ill|Tanabe Clan|ja|田辺宿禰}}, {{ill|Takehara Clan|ja|竹原氏}}, {{ill|Okura Clan|ja|巨椋連}}, {{ill|Ōkura Okihajime Clan|ja|大椋置始連}}, {{ill|Imaki Clan|ja|今木連}}, {{ill|Yuge Clan|ja|弓削氏}}, {{ill|Tame Clan|ja|多米連}}, {{ill|Minu Clan|ja|美努連}} ({{ill|Minokuni Rulers|ja|三野県主}}), {{ill|Tottoribe Clan|ja|鳥取部連}}, {{ill|Jingu Clan|ja|神宮部造}}, {{ill|Agatanoinukai Clan|ja|県犬養氏}}
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> == See Also ==

Takehazuchi no Kami is mentioned in the ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'' as ''Takehazuchi no Kami''. In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', she is referred to as {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}} and {{ill|Shidori deity|ja|倭文神}}. In the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'', she is called {{ill|Toyohachi-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波知命}} in the Yamashiro Province section and {{ill|Amenoha-no-Mikoto|ja|天破命}} in another entry. The ''Anwa Province Imbe Family Genealogy'' (安房国忌部家系), which includes the "Ibe no Sukune Honkeicho" (齋部宿祢本系帳), records her name as {{ill|Ameno Haikazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|天羽雷雄命}} and also lists {{ill|Takehazuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|武羽槌命}} and {{ill|Toyohazuji-no-Mikoto|ja|止与波豆知命}} as alternative names.<ref>{{cite book|和書 |title=安房国忌部家系|chapter=齋部宿祢本系帳|section=天羽雷雄命|doi=10.11501/2538213|url={{NDLDC|2538213/55}} }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
She is said to have woven sacred cloth (''aya'') to lure {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}} out of the {{ill|Ama-no-Iwato|ja|天の岩戸}}. The cloth she wove is called {{ill|Shidori cloth|ja|倭文布}} or simply ''Shidori'' (倭文), a term referring to a type of woven fabric. Other weaving deities include {{ill|Takuhatachichihime-no-Mikoto|ja|栲幡千々姫命}} and {{ill|Amanotatakahime-no-Mikoto|ja|天棚機姫神}}, but Takehazuchi no Kami is revered as the '''supreme ancestor of weaving'''. She is also the '''ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}'''.
She is worshipped as the '''goddess of weaving''' in '''{{ill|Shidori Shrines|ja|倭文神社}}''', '''{{ill|Shizuka Shrine|ja|静神社}}''', '''Hattori Shrines''', and others throughout Japan.
== Genealogy ==
In ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|ja|古語拾遺}}'', she is called the '''far ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori clan|ja|倭文氏}}'''. In ''{{ill|Sendai Kujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', she is described as the '''distant ancestor of the {{ill|Shidori-no-Mikoto|ja|倭文造}}'''.
== Legends and Deeds ==
Takehazuchi no Kami appears in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' under the name {{ill|Takehatsuchi-no-Mikoto|ja|建葉槌命}}. She is said to have '''subjugated the star deity''' '''{{ill|Amanomikaboshi|ja|天津甕星}} (also known as Kagaseo no Kami)''', whom '''{{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami|ja|武甕槌命}}''' and '''{{ill|Futsunushi-no-Kami|ja|経津主神}}''' were unable to pacify.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|author=植松安|date=1920-06|title=仮名日本書紀|volume=上巻|pages=87–88|publisher=大同館書店|url={{NDLDC|961006/117}} |doi=10.11501/961006 }} ({{ill|National Diet Library Digital Collections|ja|国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション}})</ref>
==Descendant clans==
{{ill|Nagahata Clan|ja|長幡部連}}, {{ill|Shidori Clan|ja|倭文氏}}, {{ill|Tanabe Clan|ja|田辺宿禰}}, {{ill|Takehara Clan|ja|竹原氏}}, {{ill|Okura Clan|ja|巨椋連}}, {{ill|Ōkura Okihajime Clan|ja|大椋置始連}}, {{ill|Imaki Clan|ja|今木連}}, {{ill|Yuge Clan|ja|弓削氏}}, {{ill|Tame Clan|ja|多米連}}, {{ill|Minu Clan|ja|美努連}} ({{ill|Minokuni Rulers|ja|三野県主}}), {{ill|Tottoribe Clan|ja|鳥取部連}}, {{ill|Jingu Clan|ja|神宮部造}}, {{ill|Agatanoinukai Clan|ja|県犬養氏}}
== Shrines ==<!--== Content from [[Shitori Shrine (Tottori)]] == --> {{ill|Takehazuchi|ja|天羽槌雄神|simple|User:Immanuelle/Takehazuchi|en:draft|8=fr}} is believed to be the ancestor of the {{Nihongo|Shitori clan|倭文氏}} of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}}<ref name="Yoshiki" />.<ref name="mizue-2" />
However, the legends of {{ill|Shitori Shrine (Tottori)|lt=Shitori Shrine|en}} are all centered around {{ill|Shitateruhime|lt=Shitateruhime-no-Mikoto|ja|シタテルヒメ|simple|User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|en:draft|8=fr}}, whom the shrine legend claims arrived in this location from {{ill|Izumo-taisha|lt=Izumo|en}} by boat. She established her residence on the mountain behind this shrine, and after her death, she was buried in a ''{{ill|kofun|en}}'' constructed in the shrine's precincts.<ref name="Yoshiki">{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}}</ref> == See Also ==
